slides.title: Reflections
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 5 English, Unit 3: Reflections. Unit 3 is called Reflections because it is all about looking closely at the world around you and expressing what you see, think, and feel through poetry and reflective writing. Poets use special tools to paint pictures with words, and by the end of this unit you will use those same tools yourself. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Identify and explain similes, metaphors, and personification in poems
Read and understand different kinds of poetry, including haiku
Count syllables and understand how poets use rhythm and sound
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to identify and explain similes, metaphors, and personification in poems. you will also read and understand different kinds of poetry, including haiku. you will also count syllables and understand how poets use rhythm and sound. Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: policy, lobby, constitution, charter, legislative, mission. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes Reflections: source text 1 and Reflections: source text 2. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?
bullets: Why They Matter Comparatives and superlatives are special forms of adjectives. They help us compare people, places, things, and ideas. When we compare...
slides.narration: The first language focus is What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?. Why They Matter Comparatives and superlatives are special forms of adjectives. They help us compare people, places, things, and ideas. When we compare poems, stories, characters, or events, we use these forms to say which is bigger, better, more beautiful, or most exciting. Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Choral Verse Performance. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
